focus: Resolve seam continuity with minimal edits.
next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 14

By the time Thea came in from town, Cora had boiled the same strip of muslin twice and still could not get the smell of camphor and old sweat out of it. The kettle sat crooked on the stove because the back leg had gone soft in the heat years ago. Every few breaths it gave a little tin complaint. Outside, the evening wind pushed dust along the yard in low, mean skirls that never rose high enough to be called a storm and never stopped long enough to let anybody forget what season it was.

Dell was in the front room on the narrow iron bed they had dragged down from the attic so he would not have to climb stairs with his ribs strapped. He ought to have been sleeping. Instead he lay half turned toward the window, his left arm thick in plaster and sling, his good hand busy with the broken alarm clock he had taken apart three days earlier. He had the little brass works spread on a flour sack beside his hip and was trying, one-handed, to settle a gear back on its post with the point of a nail. He would rather hurt himself than let a machine sit there beaten.

Cora stood in the doorway a moment with the wet muslin hanging from her fingers. His face, in the yellowed light, looked both older and younger than seventeen. The bruise along his ribs had gone from black to a rotten green at the edges. His hair needed cutting. He kept blowing it out of his eyes the way Ezra used to, sharp and impatient, as if hair itself were an insult.

"That thing won't ring if you bully it," she said.

He did not look up. "It won't ring if I leave it broke, either."

"Doctor said rest."

"Doctor says that to folks that can afford it."

He found the gear, missed the post, and swore at it under his breath. Cora crossed in, set the muslin on the chair, and lifted the flour sack by its corne...
prev_tail_excerpt:
...the bitter steam of willow bark, the faint clean trace of Thea's soap from where she had washed the vomit basin. The farm beyond the wall did not enter except as glare and demand. For the first time since Ezra's rope had cut the barn beam, Cora could imagine the land continuing without her and feel not betrayal first but relief behind it, small and monstrous and real.

She rose, went to the kitchen, and took down the blue-speckled flour tin from the top shelf. The money inside was the egg money, the sewing money, the thin little stack she had kept partitioned in her mind for feed, for note interest, for a bolt of muslin if things somehow improved enough to deserve muslin. She did not count it for the note. She counted it into her palm and back again for miles, for medicine, for whatever it cost to move a fevered boy where the next wall waiting for him was not one she had spent years patching by hand.

When she set the tin down, Thea was watching from the bedroom door.

"Cora."

The name held caution in it, and hope, and the fear of hoping out loud.

Cora slid the money back into the tin and put the lid on hard. "How long is it by rail to Lincoln?"

Thea did not answer at once. Her face changed with the effort of not reaching for this too fast. "With a change and if freight doesn't foul the schedule," she said, "most of a day. More if we miss the first connection."

Cora nodded once. She wasn't agreeing yet; she was measuring miles against money and Dell's heat.

Then she took the tin back into the bedroom and set it in the drawer beside the bed instead of on the kitchen shelf where money for the farm belonged. She sat with her hand over Dell's hot good hand and listened to him breathe. Outside, the field kept asking. For the first time, she let it ask without answering.
